Core Mechanism & Structure: TiDE is a multi-layer perceptron (MLP)-based time series model that uses residual blocks for feature encoding, temporal encoding, and decoding. It processes time series through: 1) Feature encoding of exogenous temporal features via ResBlock, 2) Concatenation of normalized input series with encoded features, 3) Multi-layer encoder ResBlocks for temporal mixing, 4) Multi-layer decoder ResBlocks for projection to output dimension, 5) Temporal decoder ResBlock that combines decoded features with future temporal features, and 6) Residual projection from input to output plus instance normalization. The architecture is fully linear/MLP without attention or recurrence.
Core Innovations: 1. **Pure MLP architecture for time series**: Uses only dense layers and residual connections, avoiding attention mechanisms for efficiency. 2. **Temporal feature encoding**: Encodes exogenous time features (like hour, day) via a dedicated ResBlock and integrates them throughout the pipeline. 3. **Residual projection**: Projects the input sequence directly to the output length via a linear layer, combined with the decoded features for final prediction.

Key Technical Details.Input Embedding: No explicit embedding; raw time series input with instance normalization. Exogenous temporal features are encoded via ResBlock.
Key Technical Details.Feature Extraction: Linear mixing via multi-layer ResBlocks (MLPs with residual connections), no attention or convolution.
Key Technical Details.Positional Encoding: No positional encoding; temporal order is implicitly captured through the sequential nature of input concatenation and residual connections.
Key Technical Details.Non-stationarity Handling: Instance normalization (mean subtraction and variance scaling) per channel in forward pass, with de-normalization at output.

Direct Observations.evidence: In forecast() and imputation() methods: means = x_enc.mean(1, keepdim=True).detach(); x_enc = x_enc - means; stdev = torch.sqrt(torch.var(x_enc, dim=1, keepdim=True, unbiased=False) + 1e-5); x_enc /= stdev

Direct Observations.claim: Uses residual blocks (ResBlock) as core building blocks
Direct Observations.evidence: Class ResBlock defined with fc1, fc2, fc3 linear layers, ReLU, dropout, and LayerNorm; used in feature_encoder, encoders, decoders, temporalDecoder

Direct Observations.claim: Supports multiple tasks: long_term_forecast, short_term_forecast, imputation
Direct Observations.evidence: Conditional branches in __init__ and forward() based on configs.task_name
feature_encoder = ResBlock(self.feature_dim, self.res_hidden, self.feature_encode_dim, dropout, bias); used in forecast() and imputation() with batch_y_mark or x_mark_enc

Direct Observations.claim: Uses residual projection from input to output
Direct Observations.evidence: self.residual_proj = nn.Linear(self.seq_len, self.pred_len, bias=bias) for forecast; added to temporalDecoder output in forecast()

Unknown / Not in Code: No RevIN (reversible instance normalization) - uses simple instance normalization

Strengths.label: Computational Efficiency
Strengths.why: Pure MLP architecture with linear layers only, no attention O(L^2) or recurrence O(L) overhead
Strengths.conditions: When sequence length L is large (e.g., >1000) and hardware favors dense matrix operations

Strengths.label: Handling Exogenous Features
Strengths.why: Dedicated feature encoder for temporal covariates (hour, day, etc.) and integration throughout model
Strengths.conditions: When rich exogenous time features are available and correlated with target series

Strengths.label: Multi-task Flexibility
Strengths.why: Architecture supports forecasting, imputation, and anomaly detection via task-specific decoder and projection heads
Strengths.conditions: When same model needs to handle different time series tasks with shared backbone

Weaknesses.label: Limited Long-range Dependency Modeling
Weaknesses.why: MLP blocks mix features globally but lack explicit temporal inductive bias like attention or recurrence
Weaknesses.conditions: When sequences have very long-term dependencies (>1000 steps) or complex temporal patterns

Weaknesses.label: Sensitivity to Input Normalization
Weaknesses.why: Uses instance normalization per forward pass; may not handle non-stationary series with distribution shift well
Weaknesses.conditions: When time series have strong non-stationarity or distribution changes over time

Weaknesses.label: Fixed-length Processing
Weaknesses.why: Architecture uses fixed seq_len and pred_len via linear projections; not inherently variable-length
Weaknesses.conditions: When input/output lengths vary significantly across samples or tasks

Resource Requirements & Complexity: Parameter scale roughly O((seq_len + feature_dim) * d_model * num_layers). Computational complexity O(B * L * d_model^2 * num_layers) for MLP operations, linear in sequence length L. Memory usage O(B * L * d_model) for activations, efficient compared to attention-based models.
Paper Abstract: Recent work has shown that simple linear models can outperform several Transformer based approaches in long term time-series forecasting. Motivated by this, we propose a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) based encoder-decoder model, Time-series Dense Encoder (TiDE), for long-term time-series forecasting that enjoys the simplicity and speed of linear models while also being able to handle covariates and non-linear dependencies. Theoretically, we prove that the simplest linear analogue of our model can achieve near optimal error rate for linear dynamical systems (LDS) under some assumptions. Empirically, we show that our method can match or outperform prior approaches on popular long-term time-series forecasting benchmarks while being 5-10x faster than the best Transformer based model.

Theoretically, we prove that the simplest linear analogue of our model can achieve near optimal error rate for linear dynamical systems (LDS) under some assumptions. Empirically, we show that our method can match or outperform prior approaches on popular long-term time-series forecasting benchmarks while being 5-10x faster than the best Transformer based model.

论文摘要: Recent work has shown that simple linear models can outperform several Transformer based approaches in long term time-series forecasting. Motivated by this, we propose a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) based encoder-decoder model, Time-series Dense Encoder (TiDE), for long-term time-series forecasting that enjoys the simplicity and speed of linear models while also being able to handle covariates and non-linear dependencies. Theoretically, we prove that the simplest linear analogue of our model can achieve near optimal error rate for linear dynamical systems (LDS) under some assumptions. Empirically, we show that our method can match or outperform prior approaches on popular long-term time-series forecasting benchmarks while being 5-10x faster than the best Transformer based model.
